subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 1 period = 40 minutes
Total: 1 periods · 40 minutes

periods.activity_title: Listening to the World Around Us: Entering Jahnavi's Story

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Listen to surroundings; do things listen back?
Jahnavi's dream; share your own dream.

phases.description: Teacher opens with the pre-reading warm-up from Let us read: students close eyes for one minute, listening to sounds, then share whether surroundings 'listen back'. Pairs discuss briefly before whole-class share-out.

Teacher introduces Jahnavi's coastal-village setting using the prose_summary — her denied schooling, her brothers Gopi and Ramu, little Appu. Students predict Jahnavi's dream and discuss how she might achieve it, connecting to their own goals.

Shared reading of the opening pages of the story. Teacher reads aloud key passages — the river's murmuring voice, Jahnavi's curiosity about nature — pausing for comprehension discussion on characterisation and setting.
Quick pair recap: students state one thing Jahnavi wants and one thing that might stop her. Teacher previews the River's role as a wise guide for the next period.

periods.teacher_notes: This period builds schema before the extract-based questions in period 2; resist moving to the question tables too early. Jahnavi's self-doubt ('I'm so old now') surfaces naturally in discussion — note it as a thread to revisit when the River responds. Students who are reluctant speakers can write their dream before sharing aloud.

periods.activity_title: River Speaks, Jahnavi Listens: Extract Analysis and Comprehension

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Complete table: Jahnavi's questions vs River's answers.
Extract 1 questions — tone, phrase, punctuation, qualities.
Extract 2 questions — girls and boys, true/false, 'slip along'.

homework.task_brief: Answer all six comprehension questions in writing.

phases.description: Brief recap of period 1: teacher asks one student to summarise Jahnavi's situation. Transition: 'Today we look closely at what the River says and how Eliza Cook writes it.'
Let us read: students complete the table (Jahnavi's questions, River's answers) in pairs, then compare with another pair. Teacher confirms answers using the prose summary.

Extract 1 and Extract 2 questions are worked through as a class: teacher reads each sub-question, cold-calls pairs, and builds answers on the board — focusing on tone (assuring vs sympathising), punctuation effect, and the phrase 'slip along'.

Teacher models how to justify answers with evidence from the text ('I chose this because the River says...') and assigns the six comprehension questions as written homework. Preview: next period addresses vocabulary and grammar.

periods.teacher_notes: The MCQ on the exclamation mark ('It couldn't be the river!') is a useful mini-lesson on how punctuation signals emotion — connect it to the story's moment of Jahnavi's surprise. The table task (Jahnavi's questions vs River's answers) also sets up the beyond-text discussion on rivers and nature in period 4.

periods.activity_title: Voices of School Life: Listening and Advice-Giving Conversations

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Circle words used by five speakers on school life.
Match statements 1–7 to speakers (i)–(v).
Speak same sentence in different emotional voices.
Ask-for and give-advice roleplay in four situations.

phases.description: Teacher hands back or recaps homework answers from period 2 briefly, then transitions: 'We have read Jahnavi's voice — now we listen to real voices talking about school.'

Let us listen (p.12, transcript p.39): teacher plays/reads the five speakers. Students first circle words from the box (task 1), then listen again to match statements to speakers (task 2). Brief pair-check before teacher confirms.

Let us speak, task 1 (p.13): teacher models the sentence 'Dear, dear! What's the matter?' in a 'happy' voice. Pairs take turns delivering the sentence in each of six emotional tones listed. Teacher circulates, prompting exaggeration of tone.

Let us speak, task 2 (p.13): pairs select one of the four advice situations (football team, Maths nerves, music practice, blackboard visibility) and rehearse an advice dialogue using the textbook prompts. Two pairs share with the class.

periods.teacher_notes: The listening task connects directly to the story's theme — speakers discuss education and gender equity, echoing Jahnavi's struggle. For the speaking task, ensure quieter students have a role; the structured prompts in task 2 lower the barrier to participation for hesitant speakers.
materials: Audio resource or teacher-read transcript p.39

periods.activity_title: Sound Words, Loanwords, and River Wisdom: Vocabulary and Exploration

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Why 'River' is capitalised — personification discussion.
Match sound words to definitions; fill-in-blank sentences.
Discuss why Indian rivers are considered sacred.

homework.task_brief: Research meanings and origins of eight loanwords.

phases.description: Let us learn task 1 (p.8): teacher asks why 'River' has a capital letter and guides students to the concept of personification — the River speaks, listens, and laughs like a person. Students connect to earlier extract work.

Let us learn task 2 (p.8–9): students work individually to match nine sound words (shrieked, splashed, murmuring, etc.) to definitions, then fill in the six blanks in the classroom scenario. Teacher corrects on board.

Let us explore task 1 (p.14): brief whole-class discussion — why are Indian rivers considered sacred and female? Teacher draws on the prose summary's river imagery (the wise, affectionate River) to anchor the cultural connection. Two or three student contributions noted on the board.
Teacher assigns loanword research (karma, sahib, chutney, etc.) as homework and previews the writing task next period.

periods.teacher_notes: The capitalisation of 'River' is a natural bridge from literary device to grammar — it shows how grammar choices carry meaning. The sacred-rivers discussion (beyond-text) is kept short here; if students raise further questions, direct them to the homework research on loanwords from Indian languages.

periods.activity_title: Describing Nature: Observation Paragraph Planning and Drafting

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Write descriptive paragraph on observed nature; give title.

phases.description: Teacher recaps the story's nature imagery from the prose summary — spiders in yellow flowers, bamboo rustling, moonrise behind hills. Students share one nature detail they noticed this week (from any prior observation).

Let us write (p.14): teacher models a brief planning frame on the board — title, colour, shape, texture, unique detail. Students spend five minutes making a bullet-point plan of their observations before writing.

Independent writing: students draft their descriptive paragraph using their plan. Teacher circulates, prompting with questions ('What texture? What colour exactly? Which detail was most surprising?') and encouraging specific, sensory language.
Two volunteers read their opening sentence aloud. Class gives one compliment and one suggestion each. Teacher explains that revision will happen in their own time and collects drafts for feedback.

periods.teacher_notes: This is Section A's only writing task and draws directly on the story's rich nature descriptions — connect student observations back to how Cook uses nature imagery to mirror Jahnavi's inner world. Collect drafts at the end; written feedback (one strength, one improvement) can be returned before Section C's writing period.

periods.activity_title: Bruce and the Spider: Reading the Poem and Unpacking Perseverance

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Identify pictures; what can we learn from them?
Fill blanks to complete poem summary.
Complete sentences on narrative style and central idea.

phases.description: Pre-reading (p.18): pairs examine the textbook pictures and share what they see and what lesson can be drawn. Teacher bridges to the poem's context — a Scottish king in despair watching a spider.

Shared reading of the full poem aloud; teacher reads a stanza, then students echo-read the next. Emphasis on the tone shift from 'low despair' to 'Bravo, bravo!' — drawing on the poem_appreciation_summary's description of the mood arc.

Let us discuss tasks 1 and 2 (p.19): students individually fill the summary blanks (tried, conquered, mounted, etc.) then in pairs complete sentences on narrative style and central idea. Pairs share answers; teacher corrects and consolidates on board.
Exit check: teacher asks two students to identify one example of the spider's effort and name the poem's rhyme scheme. Preview: next period moves to extract questions and literary devices.

periods.teacher_notes: The ballad-like ABAB rhyme scheme and the arc from despair to triumph are central to appreciation — use the poem_appreciation_summary's note on the tone shift at 'Ah me! 'tis an anxious minute' to show students how suspense is built. This period also transitions from Section A's prose style to verse, so briefly discuss the difference in form.
materials: Poem text displayed on board or projected

periods.activity_title: Metaphors, Similes, and Spider Words: Deep Reading and Language Work

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Three examples each: spider's efforts and alliteration.
Why repeat 'tried and tried', 'steadily, steadily', 'up, up'?
Identify metaphors (M) and similes (S) in sentences.
Write opposites: up, fast, glad, win, succeed.
Match word meanings; frame sentences for each.

phases.description: Let us think and reflect tasks 3 and 4 (p.19): students find three spider-effort lines and three alliteration examples, then discuss why repetition ('tried and tried', 'steadily, steadily') intensifies the poem's emotional effect. Answers noted on board.

Task 6 (p.20): teacher introduces metaphor vs simile using two examples from the textbook, then students work in pairs to label all nine sentences M or S. Whole-class check; teacher highlights how the spider's climb is itself a metaphor for the King's struggle.

Let us learn tasks 1 and 2 (pp.22–23): students write opposites of five poem words, then match four vocabulary items (endeavour, toil, strive, braced) to their definitions and frame original sentences. Teacher circulates.

Pairs share their sentences for one vocabulary word each. Teacher gives brief feedback on accuracy of usage, then previews the extract-based questions and listening/speaking tasks for section B in period 8.

periods.teacher_notes: Alliteration examples in the poem ('silken, filmy'; 'delicate thread it had to tread') are identified in the poem_appreciation_summary — use them as anchors before students search independently. The metaphor/simile work in this period directly supports the writing task in period 8.

periods.activity_title: Ant Story, Narration, Letter of Perseverance, and Spider Web Project

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Guess answers to ant story questions before listening.
Listen again; check whether guesses are correct.
Narrate personal experience of motivation and perseverance.
Plan and begin letter to cousin on overcoming difficulty.
Observe spider vs ant; note differences; share observations.

homework.task_brief: Complete letter draft using transition words.

phases.description: Let us listen (p.24, transcript p.40): teacher reads the guiding questions first; students predict answers. Teacher then reads/plays the ant story and students check their predictions. Whole-class verification of MCQ answers.

Let us speak (p.25): teacher models the narration structure using the sentence prompts on the board (introduction, challenge, motivation, action, result, reflection). Two students narrate briefly; class gives one positive observation each.

Let us write (p.26): teacher explains the letter format and transition words (To begin with…, Next…, Finally…). Students plan their letter in bullet points (steps to overcome difficulty; advice to cousin). Teacher circulates and prompts.

Let us explore task 2 (p.26): brief whole-class discussion — how are spiders and ants different? Students note 2–3 differences (body parts, number of legs, silk-spinning). Teacher mentions the spider web craft as a take-home option. Homework: complete the letter draft.

periods.teacher_notes: This period covers four spines in one 50-minute block — keep each segment tightly timed. The letter task is begun in class and completed as homework; the transition-word requirement connects to the functional-writing pedagogy strand. The ant story in the listening task thematically mirrors the spider poem, which helps students make the connection without explicit prompting.
materials: Three sticks and wool (optional, for spider web craft take-home)

periods.activity_title: Helen Keller's Gift of Three Days: Reading and Assistive Technology Listening

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Identify true statements from seven about Helen Keller.
Complete three-day table of what Keller would do.
Eye as 'window of the soul' — how Keller reads feelings.
Select four true statements from mother-son conversation.

phases.description: Pre-reading warm-up (p.28): students recall a place they visited and describe one thing they could see, hear, smell, taste, and touch. Teacher introduces Helen Keller — blind since childhood — asking students to imagine losing one sense.

Shared reading of key passages from the informational text (pp.28–33), guided by the prose_summary: Keller's tactile world, her three-day wish, and the exhortation to use senses fully. Students then work in pairs on true/false task (p.31) and the three-day table.

Comprehension discussion: teacher asks 'How does Keller understand feelings without sight?' — students locate the 'window of the soul' passage (p.32) and construct a brief written answer. Two responses shared aloud.

Let us listen (p.36, transcript p.41): teacher reads/plays the mother-son conversation on Braille and assistive technology; students select four true statements from the seven listed. Answers confirmed before period ends.

periods.teacher_notes: The prose summary notes that Keller's tone is 'reflective, eloquent, and gently instructive' — contrast this with the narrative prose of Section A and the poem of Section B so students see how informational writing differs. The listening task on Braille and Digital India directly connects to the beyond-text tasks in period 10.

periods.activity_title: Senses, Modal Verbs, Descriptive Writing, and Braille Exploration

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Speak for one minute on the sense you value most.
Complete sensory words table from text; add new words.
Identify modal verbs; choose functions in sentences.
Plan and write descriptive paragraph on a visited place.
Write name using Braille code; use bindis or daal.

phases.description: Let us speak (p.36): three or four students take turns speaking for one minute on the sense they value most, using the textbook prompts. Peers note one strong phrase from each speaker.

Let us learn tasks 1 and 2 (pp.34–35): students complete the sensory words table (what you see/hear/smell/taste/feel) from the text, then identify modal verbs in five sentences and match each to its function (possibility, necessity, polite request, ability, moral obligation).

Let us write (p.37): teacher briefly models the three-part descriptive paragraph structure (interesting opener, five-sense details with alliteration/simile/personification, summary conclusion). Students plan and write independently. Teacher circulates.

Let us explore task 1 (p.37): students study the Braille alphabet and write their name using bindis or daal on paper. Teacher closes the chapter by connecting Helen Keller's message — use every sense fully — back to Jahnavi's courage and Bruce's perseverance as the chapter's unifying theme.

periods.teacher_notes: This final period compresses four spines; keep the speaking slot brief and the writing task as the central block. The Braille activity is a concrete, tactile beyond-text experience that directly enacts Keller's argument about touch as knowledge — a powerful closing activity. Return Section A writing drafts with feedback before or during this period.
materials: Bindis or daal grains for Braille name activity

items.source_spine_section: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect
items.prompt: Complete the table given below with Jahnavi's questions in Column 1 and the River's answers in Column 2.
items.visual_stimulus:
Column 1|Column 2
...why the moon always comes from behind the hills…?|[blank]
[blank]|… little girls can do as much as little boys.
[blank]|Big boats, so big that they can take hundreds of people, and they sail along the sea with lights that shine all night.
Will they come here?|[blank]

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: Row 1: The River's answer — the moon always rises from behind the hills because that is where its home is (the River does not give a direct scientific answer but reassures Jahnavi with wonder). Row 2: Jahnavi's question — Can I do something? / Is there any difference between girls and boys? Row 3: Jahnavi's question — What are ships? / Tell me about ships. Row 4: The River's answer — Yes, they come this way and pass by, sailing on the sea beyond.

items.source_spine_section: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect
items.prompt: Complete the summary of the poem by filling in the blanks with suitable words from the box: tried, conquered, mounted, low, despair, strive, attempts, great. The poem tells the story of King Bruce of Scotland, who was feeling 1. _____ after failing multiple times to achieve something 2. _____ for his people. In a moment of 3. _____, he observed a spider trying to reach its web. The spider 4. _____ every time it fell after getting close. It made nine 5. _____ and finally succeeded. The king said the spider had 6. _____ and so would he. Inspired by the spider's determination, King Bruce decided to 7. _____. When he 8. _____ this time, he ultimately succeeded.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. low  2. great  3. despair  4. mounted  5. attempts  6. conquered  7. strive  8. tried

items.source_spine_section: Let us read + Let us discuss + Let us think and reflect
items.prompt: Work in pairs. Recall a visit to a place you liked. Discuss and write: (1) Name of the place (2) The person(s) you went with (3) Things you could: see, listen, smell, taste, touch and feel.

expected_elements: Names the place and companions clearly.
Describes at least three different senses with specific details.
Speaks in complete sentences at a steady, audible pace.
Connects the sensory memory to a personal feeling or response.

items.prompt: Read the words in the box. Listen to five people speak about school life. Circle the words used by the speakers. (There are two words you do not need.) Words: lawyer, newspapers, marriage, desks, homemaker, advertisements, principal, careers, posters, books.

expected_elements: Listens attentively and circles words heard, not assumed.
Identifies eight correct words and leaves two unused words unmarked.
Can name the two unused words when asked by teacher.
Demonstrates active listening by not guessing ahead of the audio.

items.prompt: Listen to a story about an ant. Before listening, guess the answers: (1) What was the ant known for? (i) determination (ii) strength (iii) wisdom. (2) Why was the ant unable to take the roti home? (i) It was stuck under a rock. (ii) It was too heavy to carry. (iii) It was pulled by other ants. (3) What was the bird doing? (i) playing with the ant (ii) searching for food (iii) picking up leaves. (4) How did the bird make the ant feel? (i) proud (ii) inspired (iii) relaxed. (5) How did the fellow ants help? (i) by advising the ant continuously (ii) by standing still near the ant (iii) by joining the ant in her efforts.

options.text: (ii) It was too heavy to carry.
(iii) by joining the ant in her efforts

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1-(i) determination; 2-(ii) It was too heavy to carry; 3-(ii) searching for food; 4-(ii) inspired; 5-(iii) by joining the ant in her efforts.

items.prompt: Listen to a conversation between a mother and son. Select four true statements from: (1) Anuj finds the concept of Braille fascinating and wants to learn more. (2) Mother is unsure of the school's efforts to include students with visual impairment. (3) Anuj initially thinks that Digital India is only about learning how to use computers. (4) Mother believes that screen readers are very effective for persons who are visually impaired. (5) Anuj already knew that persons who are visually impaired can use computers and smartphones. (6) Anuj wants to know about the effectiveness of assistive technologies. (7) Mother believes a right mindset is the best support for persons who are visually impaired.

options.text: Anuj finds the concept of Braille fascinating and wants to learn more.
Mother is unsure of the school's efforts to include students with visual impairment.
Anuj initially thinks that Digital India is only about learning how to use computers.
Mother believes that screen readers are very effective for persons who are visually impaired.
Anuj already knew that persons who are visually impaired can use computers and smartphones.
Anuj wants to know about the effectiveness of assistive technologies.
Mother believes a right mindset is the best support for persons who are visually impaired.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: The four true statements are: 1, 3, 4, and 7.

items.prompt: Work in pairs and take turns to speak the sentence 'Dear, dear! What's the matter?...' in different voices: happy, surprised, angry, scared, sad, worried.

expected_elements: Delivers the sentence in at least three clearly distinguishable tones.
Uses appropriate facial expression and volume to match each voice.
Takes turns without interrupting partner.
Can name the tone they are using when prompted by teacher.

items.prompt: Narrate a personal experience about a time when you were motivated by someone or something to carry on and never give up. Use the given sentence prompts as a guide: Introduction (I remember a time when I felt like giving up...), Describe the challenge, Feeling disheartened, The source of motivation, Taking action, The result, Reflection.

expected_elements: Follows the seven-part structure provided in the prompts.
Identifies a specific source of motivation (person, event, or object).
Uses past tense consistently and speaks in complete sentences.
Reflects on what the experience taught them about perseverance.

items.prompt: Work in pairs. Take turns to speak for a minute on the sense you value the most. Use prompts: I value my sense of sight/sound/touch/smell/taste the most because... One of my favourite experiences is... This is so because... I appreciate being able to see/hear/touch/smell/taste... God forbid, if I were to lose this sense, it would make me feel... I would not be able to... I thank God that I am able to...

expected_elements: Clearly identifies one specific sense and gives a personal reason.
Uses at least two prompts from the textbook to structure the response.
Speaks for approximately one minute at a steady pace.
Makes eye contact and avoids reading directly from notes.

items.prompt: Observe the natural world around you over the next few days — colours, shapes, patterns, textures of plants, trees, flowers, birds, the sky. Record your observations. Then write a descriptive paragraph based on what you've observed. Give a title to your paragraph.

expected_elements: Gives a clear, relevant title that reflects the content.
Describes at least three sensory details (colour, texture, shape, or sound).
Uses specific adjectives and avoids vague words like 'nice' or 'good'.
Writes in coherent sentences with a clear opening and closing.

items.prompt: Think about the steps you can take to overcome a difficult situation. Write a letter to your cousin on how you plan to overcome any difficult situation, advising not to quit and persevere. Use transition words like 'To begin with…', 'Next…', 'After that…', 'Then...', 'Finally...'.

expected_elements: Uses correct informal letter format (date, greeting, body, closing).
Outlines at least three steps using the required transition words.
Includes specific advice to the cousin about not giving up.
Maintains a warm, encouraging tone appropriate for a personal letter.

items.prompt: Write a descriptive paragraph describing a place you visited recently. (1) Begin with an interesting opening line introducing the place. (2) In the supporting lines, describe the place using adjectives and details for all five senses — sight, sound, smell, taste, touch — including alliterations, similes, personifications. (3) In the concluding lines, summarise and mention the most important thing about the place.

expected_elements: Opens with an engaging sentence that names and introduces the place.
Includes at least one alliteration, one simile, or one personification.
Covers at least three of the five senses with specific detail.
Concludes by identifying what makes the place memorable or unique.

items.prompt: Why has the author used a capital letter for the 'River' even when it is a common noun? (Clue: Has the River been given qualities of a human being?)

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: The author capitalises 'River' because the River has been personified — it speaks, listens, laughs, and gives advice like a wise human being, so it is treated as a proper name rather than a common noun.

items.prompt: Write the opposites of the following words taken from the poem: (1) up × _____ (2) fast × _____ (3) glad × _____ (4) win × _____ (5) succeed × _____.

Complete the table with sensory words from the text and add new words. Senses: What you see (panorama), What you hear (music), What you smell (perfume), What you taste (relish), What you feel (rough).
items.visual_stimulus:
Sense|From text|New words
What you see|panorama|
What you hear|music|
What you smell|perfume|
What you taste|relish|
What you feel|rough|

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: Sight — panorama, smooth, gleaming (accept any textbook sight-words); Hearing — music, whisper, rustle (accept textbook hearing-words); Smell — perfume, fragrant (accept textbook smell-words); Taste — relish, savour (accept textbook taste-words); Touch — rough, soft, delicate, smooth bark/birch skin (accept textbook touch-words). New words column: any appropriate sensory adjective accepted.

items.prompt: Discuss why rivers in India are considered sacred and most are referred to as female. Some rivers are referred to as male — for example, the Brahmaputra and the Sone.

expected_elements: Mentions at least one reason rivers are considered sacred in India (life-giving, used in rituals, etc.).
Notes the grammatical/cultural reason most rivers are female (e.g., nurturing, motherly role).
Gives at least one example of a female river name (Ganga, Yamuna, Kaveri, etc.).
Acknowledges that male river names also exist and provides an example.

items.prompt: Collect folk songs in your own language that connect us with nature and compile them as a class project.

expected_elements: Contributes at least one folk song with its title and language.
Briefly describes the nature theme or imagery in the song.
Compiled collection is presented neatly on chart paper or in a booklet.
Student can sing or recite at least a few lines of their contributed song.

items.prompt: Study the Braille alphabet code and write your name using it. You may use bindis or grains of daal to make the dots that you can touch and feel.

expected_elements: Correctly translates each letter of their name into the Braille dot pattern.
Creates a tactile representation using bindis, daal, or similar materials.
Can explain one fact about how Braille is read (by touch, raised dots, etc.).
Connects the activity to Helen Keller's description of touch as a sense of knowledge.
